coredata.dc:title: Rapid visualized assessment of tetracycline in actual fruit samples: The combination of perovskite and rare earth complexes
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jun
Li, Rui
Xu, Zhitao
Chen, Xiangzhen
Li, Yongxin
Gou, Jian
Zhang, Lina
Bi, Ning
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: The abuse of tetracycline (TC) has resulted in serious environmental pollution and the preparation of highly sensitive fluorescent probes capable of rapidly detecting TC has become a hot research field. All-inorganic halide perovskites have attracted extensive because of their good fluorescent properties, while the instability in polar solvents hinders its development for practical applications. In the present study, a perovskite quantum dot that can emit green fluorescence was prepared, and a ratio nanocomposite fluorescent probe (CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu) for the rapid visual assessment of TC in ethanol was obtained through functional modification by rare earth complexes. The continuous addition of TC led to the distinct color transition from green to red, resulting in the visualized multicolor sensing for TC. The probe had a good linear relationship with detection limit of 12.1 nM. The sensing mechanism research indicated that the quenching of perovskite nanocrystals in the probe was caused by the PET effect, and the red fluorescence enhancement was caused by the “antenna effect” produced by the chelation of TC with Eu3+. In addition, a portable wearable device for the fast visual detection of TC was developed to realize the rapid and intelligent detection of TC in actual fruit samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite
Tetracycline
Rare earth complexes
Intelligent detection
Actual fruit samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of agarose-based portable wearable device
Optimum experimental conditions of TC analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1904168 , No. 51773052 and No. 21805071 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan ( 222300420047 ), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015 ), and the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University ( T2021-2 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2 during (a) and after (b) formation. (c)TEM mapping image of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2, (d) CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu, (e) High resolution TEM image of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu, (f) TEM mapping images of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu.
(a) XRD spectra of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6. (b) XRD patterns of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2 and CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu.Core-level XPS spectrum of (c) Pb 4f, (d) Br 3d, (e) Cs 3d, and (f) Eu 3d

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu to different concentrations of TC, (b) piecewise fitting linear relationship between I616/I520 and TC (0–5 μM and 5–25 μM), (c) CIE chromaticity diagram in the presence of different concentrations of TC, (d) Photographs of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu with different TC viewed under UV light at 365 nm.

Selectivity of the proposed probe against cations and physiological materials (a), anions (b), and antibiotics (d). Fluorescence photos of the response of related interfering analytes to CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (c).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the probe before (green solid line) and after (red dotted line) adding TC, (b) energy transfer mechanism diagram of the probe, (c) fluorescence lifetimes of the probe before and after adding TC in ethanol and heavy water, (d) fluorescence lifetimes of the probe corresponding to different concentrations of TC, (e) fluorescence spectrum of the probe and the UV–vis spectrum of TC, (f) UV–vis spectrum of TC and UV–vis spectra of the probe before and after adding TC.
Visual detection of TC by gloved portable fluorescence sensor.
Preparation of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu nanosensor and visualized testing schematic of TC.

head.ce:title: Rapid visualized assessment of tetracycline in actual fruit samples: The combination of perovskite and rare earth complexes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abuse of tetracycline (TC) has resulted in serious environmental pollution and the preparation of highly sensitive fluorescent probes capable of rapidly detecting TC has become a hot research field. All-inorganic halide perovskites have attracted extensive because of their good fluorescent properties, while the instability in polar solvents hinders its development for practical applications. In the present study, a perovskite quantum dot that can emit green fluorescence was prepared, and a ratio nanocomposite fluorescent probe (CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu) for the rapid visual assessment of TC in ethanol was obtained through functional modification by rare earth complexes. The continuous addition of TC led to the distinct color transition from green to red, resulting in the visualized multicolor sensing for TC. The probe had a good linear relationship with detection limit of 12.1 nM. The sensing mechanism research indicated that the quenching of perovskite nanocrystals in the probe was caused by the PET effect, and the red fluorescence enhancement was caused by the “antenna effect” produced by the chelation of TC with Eu3+. In addition, a portable wearable device for the fast visual detection of TC was developed to realize the rapid and intelligent detection of TC in actual fruit samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) antibiotics are a broad-spectrum antibiotic with phenanthrene nucleus in its structure, and they have been widely used in various bacterial, rickettsia, mycoplasma, and other infections (e.g., chlamydia) [1]. Considering the low price and broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity of TC, its use is increasing rapidly worldwide [2–4]. However, the excessive and illegal use of TCs cause negative effects such as water pollution, bacterial resistance, and food safety [5–7]. Aside from causing serious chemical pollution, excessive TC drug residues can enrich drug-resistant bacteria in the environment and the production of resistance genes, causing a certain degree of drug resistance in the human body through environmental transmission [8–10]. Therefore, many new methods for TC detection have been developed for its abuse, including electrochemical analysis [11], chemiluminescence method [12], colorimetric method [13], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [14]. Although the above methods can realize the sensitive detection of TC, they also have the disadvantages of long time, complex instruments, professional operation, and complex sample preparation process compared with the fluorescence method [15–19]. Fluorescence method has the characteristics of simple operation, intuitive phenomenon, visual tracking observation, and strong specificity, making it widely used in the field of TC residue detection. However, most of the fluorescence sensors based on TC detection have a single emission fluorescence response. Considering the lack of fluorescence internal standard, the color change is single and cannot be easily identified by the naked eyes. The signal is also susceptible to the interference of sensor concentration, noise, and other factors, thus reducing the detection sensitivity to a certain extent. The quantitative analysis of ratio fluorescent probes is based on the ratio of the emission intensities of the two fluorescent groups, allowing this method to eliminate external environmental interference and to calibrate and improve its accuracy [20]. Among the numerous ratio fluorescence detection methods, fluorescence detection based on nanoparticles has become an applicable method because of its outstanding characteristics such as high sensitivity, good selectivity, strong diversity, and low cost [21–23]. In recent years, extensive research on traditional fluorescent materials such as nanoparticles [24], quantum dots [25–28], carbon dots [29,30], and metal-organic frameworks [31] has made fluorescence technology an important means to detect various chemical, biological, and drug molecules. Metal halide perovskite has become a kind of material with great development potential. Perovskite nanocrystals, as new types of fluorescent materials, have attracted extensive attention in the field of luminescence because of their large absorption coefficient, high luminescence quantum yield, and easy adjustment of the band gap [32–37]. Their good photoluminescence properties are also very suitable as a fluorescence internal standard. With the in-depth study of perovskite fluorescent sensors, they have shown many potential applications for the detection of conserving oil quality [32], heavy metal ions [33,34], anions [35], and small molecules [36–38]. However, considering the poor stability and easy hydrolysis of perovskite nanocrystals in polar solvents, their development in analytical determination is greatly limited. In addition, perovskite-based ratio fluorescent probes are rarely reported.

Lanthanide luminescent ions can usually be sensitized by organic luminescent groups with specific photophysical properties through “antenna effect” (AE) [39]. These luminescent groups known as antennas are embedded in ligands, which can stabilize trivalent lanthanide ions and form dynamic and thermodynamically stable complexes [40]. Some luminescent groups with strong absorption transfer the absorbed energy to lanthanide ions, resulting in AE and sensitizing the luminescence of lanthanide ions. The β-diketone structure, large conjugated system, and high molar absorption coefficient of TC increases the π-π* transition probability. In addition, functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carbon-carbon double bonds on TC easily coordinate with lanthanide ions to form stable chelates [40]. In this process, the energy absorbed by TC can sensitize lanthanide ions to emit fluorescence with characteristic colors [41]. Therefore, the multicolor fluorescence assay and analysis of TC can be realized by combining the unique antenna effect of lanthanide metals with the fluorescence characteristics of perovskite itself.

Based on the above research background, a ratiometric fluorescent nanocomposited probe (Scheme 1 ) for the detection of TC in ethanol was prepared. First, the Cs4PbBr6 shell matrix was formed on the surface of perovskite nanocrystals CsPbBr3 nanocrystals (NCs), and this structure can protect the fluorescence of CsPbBr3 NCs from being stable in solvent. After the formation of core-shell perovskite CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6, 3- aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) in the solution was hydrolyzed into silica, and the surface of the nanocrystals was modified by the corresponding amino groups (CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2). The amino groups attached to the surface not only enabled the perovskite nanocrystals to be uniformly dispersed in polar solvents, but also facilitated the covalent grafting of subsequent europium complexes. At this time, the amino-rich perovskite quantum dots on the surface can be functionalized by citric acid (Cit) and rare earth europium (Eu) through amidation to obtain CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu multicolor fluorescent nanosensors. When TC was added into the detection system, the green fluorescence of perovskite quantum dots gradually decreased, whereas the red fluorescence of rare earth europium gradually increased. The color of whole system showed a notable green-to-red transformation, which realized the multicolor sensing for TC. Accordingly, a portable wearable device for rapid visual detection of TC was developed to realize the rapid and intelligent detection of TC in actual fruit samples.
All chemicals and characterization are provided in the Supporting Information.

According to the method in the literature [42], 0.16 mmol cesium bromide and 0.2 mmol lead bromide were dissolved in 5 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide solution to form the precursor, which was ultrasonically treated until the precursor solution was clear and transparent. Approximately 1 mL of the prepared precursor solution was added to 20 mL of toluene solution, and 200 μL of oleylamine, 250 μL of oleic acid, and 250 μL of APTES were added in batches. After separating the solids by centrifugation at 25 °C for 10 h, the final CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2 powder was obtained by vacuum drying.

The previously obtained white solid powder of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2 was added to the activated citric acid solution in batches. After stirring for 20 h at room temperature, the solid was separated by centrifugation. The obtained CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2 solid and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (20 mg) were re-dispersed in 15 mL of ethanol, and then stirred for 8 h at room temperature. The final product was washed thrice with water and dried for further use.

Approximately 300 μL of ethanol solution of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu fluorescent probe with a concentration of 1 mg/mL was added to the colorimetric dish, and the final solution was set to 2 mL. The fluorescence titration method was used to test the response of the probe to TC, and the results were recorded in the steady-state fluorescence emission spectrum under 370 nm excitation. The detection performance of the composited nanoprobe for TC was investigated by testing the fluorescence intensity of the probe at different TC concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of agarose-based portable wearable device
ce:section.ce:para: 250 mg of agarose and 50 mg of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu fluorescent biosensor were dispersed in 10 mL and 2 mL buffer solutions, respectively. The above substances were mixed and homogenized in an oil bath at 100 °C for 10 min, then cooled to room temperature and placed in a mold for 12 h to obtain an “L” -shaped agarose gel multicolor fluorescence biosensor. Agarose containing nano-fluorescent probes can be fixed on the surface of rubber gloves to prepare a portable wearable device for rapid visual detection of TC.

The prepared CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu fluorescent probe was studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in the SEM image in Fig. 1 a, during the formation of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2, the periphery of perovskite was wrapped by silica. Fig. 1b shows the SEM images of the final CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2. The morphology was spherical with uniform distribution, and the average particle size was approximately 0.27 μm, because the Si–O–C2H5 group in APTES can be hydrolyzed into the SiO2 shell coated on the surface of the perovskite nanocrystals. This structure can also prevent polar molecules from damaging perovskite nanocrystals, while the Cs4PbBr6 matrix and SiO2 shell provided double protection for CsPbBr3 nanocrystals to maintain their stable optical properties [42]. Fig. 1c shows the TEM mapping image of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6. Moreover, Pb, Cs, Br and other elements were evenly distributed, confirming the successful preparation of perovskite nanocrystals. Fig. 1d illustrates that the morphology of perovskite nanocrystals coated with SiO2 did not change after the grafting of Eu complexes, indicating that the amidation reaction and rare earth europium coordination occurred on the surface of SiO2 shell. The high-resolution of TEM image in Fig. 1e shows the lattice fringes of the (214) crystal plane of perovskite, and the crystal plane spacing was approximately 0.312 nm [43]. The TEM mapping images of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu in Fig. 1f indicates that Pb, Cs, Br, C, O, Eu, and other elements were evenly distributed. The EDX spectrum in Fig. S1 also demonstrates that the fluorescent probe contained C, O, Pb, Br, Cs, Eu, and other elements, confirming the successful preparation of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu.

The formation process of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu probe can be further explained by analyzing the XRD diffraction pattern. Fig. 2 a shows the XRD pattern of core-shell perovskite nanocrystals CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6. The green-labelled diffraction peaks represented the formation of CsPbBr3 nanocrystals. The main diffraction peaks were concentrated at 15.21°, 21.50°, 26.35°, 30.70°, 34.20°, 37.60°, and 43.70°, corresponding to the (100), (110), (111), (200), (210), (211) and (202) crystal planes of perovskite nanocrystals, respectively, which belong to the cubic structure of CsPbBr3 (PDF No. 18–0364) [44]. At the same time, the characteristic diffraction peaks at 12.89°, 18.08°, 20.08°, 22.41°, 25.43°, 28.60°, 30.27°, 34.15°, and 38.99° correspond to the (110), (202), (113), (300), (024), (214), (223), and (134) crystal planes of Cs4PbBr6, which belong to the diamond matrix structure of Cs4PbBr6 (PDF No. 73–2478) [45]. Fig. 2b shows the XRD diffraction pattern of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6 after chemical modification. After coating amorphous silica, a coating peak was observed at 20°–30°.After grafting the Cit-Eu complex, the position of the characteristic peak in XRD did not shift, but the peak intensity was slightly lower than that without grafting Cit-Eu complex, indicating that the grafting did not destroy the internal structure of the fluorescent probe [46].

The elemental composition and valence state of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu were studied in detail by XPS. As illustrated in Fig. S2(a), the peaks at 74.4, 143.2, 282.9, 436.0, 530.1, 740.4, 1136.7, and 1164.6 eV corresponded to Br 3d, Pb 4f, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Cs 3d, Eu 3d5, and Eu 3d3, respectively, indicating that the Eu complex was successfully loaded on the surface of perovskite nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 2c, the lead level in the high-resolution XPS spectra had two peaks at 138.87 and 143.74 eV, which belonged to Pb 3d7/2 and Pb 3d5/2 [50]. Fig. 2d indicates that the two peaks at 68.44 and 69.41 eV can be attributed to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 [50]. The Cs 3d spectrum in Fig. 2e exhibited two contributions at 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 (resulting from the spin-orbit splitting), which are located at 723.66 and 737.60 eV, respectively [51]. The C1s spectrum in Fig. S2b shows that four characteristic peaks were assigned to C–C/C–H, C–N, C–O, and CO bonds in CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu [49]. The O1s in Fig. S2c proved the formation of OC, O–C, and O–Si bonds [52]. As illustrated in Fig. 2f, four peaks appeared after loading europium complexes, which is caused by the multiple splitting of the spin orbital j = 3/2 and 5/2 energies of europium. The peaks at 1124.94 and 1154.85 eV belonged to Eu2+ 3d5/2 and Eu2+ 3d3/2, and the peaks at 1135.03 and 1165.01 eV belonged to Eu3+ 3d5/2 and Eu3+ 3d3/2 [48].

Fig. S2(d) shows the FTIR spectra of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6 after chemical modification. The peak at 946 cm−1 indicated the tensile vibration peak of Si–OH formed by APTES hydrolysis. The vibration peak of Si–O–C at 1034 cm−1 and the vibration peak of Si–O–Si at 1130 cm−1 indicated the appearance of SiO2 shell [46]. The peak positions at 1543 cm−1 and 3438 cm−1 represented the in-plane bending vibration and tensile vibration of N–H, and the peak position at 1620 cm−1 represented the out-of-plane bending vibration of N–H [47]. The peaks at 2855 cm−1 and 2928 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of C–H [48]. After the amidation reaction, the peak at 1400 cm−1 represented the vibration peak of CO in the carboxyl group, proving the successful grafting of europium complex [49]. Therefore, the europium citrate complex (Cit-Eu) was successfully grafted onto the surface of SiO2-coated perovskite nanocrystals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum experimental conditions of TC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The optimum experimental conditions of TC sensing were determined by studying the excitation spectra of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu, the emission spectra at different initial probe concentrations, and the stability in different solvents. First, the excitation spectra of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu was measured, as shown in Fig. S3a. In the excitation wavelength range of 350–390 nm, the emission peak of the composited probe remained at 520 nm, but the fluorescence intensity changed. When the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu reached the maximum. Therefore, the best excitation of the probe was 370 nm, under which subsequent tests were carried out. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the composited probe in different polar solvents was tested. As shown in Fig. S3b, in the DMF solvent, the peak position of the composite probe notably had a red shift, and the fluorescence intensity was reduced; this phenomenon can be attributed to the dehydration and condensation reaction between the carbonyl group in DMF solvent and the amino group outside the perovskite-Eu nanosensor [53].

The initial concentration and stability of the fluorescent probe CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu during TC detection were also studied. As shown in Fig. S4a, when 0.15 mg/mL of the sensor was added, the corresponding fluorescence value was the largest. When less than 0.15 mg/mL of the sensor was added, low fluorescence intensity was observed. When the addition amount was greater than 0.15 mg/mL, the constant amount of solvent caused an increase in the concentration of the sensor, which may lead to uneven dispersion in ethanol and thus affecting the visual detection of TC. Therefore, 0.15 mg/mL was selected as the optimal initial concentration of the sensor. The response of fluorescent probe to TC within 0–1 min was tested to explore the optimal response time of fluorescent probe to TC. As shown in Fig. S4b, when the reaction time was 20 s, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I
616 /I
520 ) of the fluorescent probe at 616 and 520 nm reached the maximum and remained stable within 1 min. The time stability of the fluorescent probe under high-pressure xenon lamp irradiation for 800 s was also researched. Fig. S4c shows the fluorescence intensity ratio at excitation wavelengths of 520 and 616 nm, indicating that CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu had good optical stability. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu in ethanol within 180 days was measured to investigate the long-term fluorescence stability of the probe. As shown in Fig. S4d, the fluorescence intensity can still be maintained at approximately 90%.

The above results proved that the perfect combination of perovskite and Eu complexes had strong fluorescence stability, which was expected to be used for visual analysis of TC. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu decreased at 520 nm and increased at 616 nm with the addition of TC. As shown in Fig. 3b, in the linear sections of 0–5 and 5–25 μM, the addition amount of TC showed a good progressive relationship with the intensity ratio of the fluorescence characteristic peaks at 616 and 520 nm. The linear regression indexes were 0.9951 (0–5 μM) and 0.9944 (5–25 μM). The detection limit is calculated by using the equation of 3 times signal-to-noise ratio 3δ/k (where δ is the relative standard deviation of repeated measurement results, k is the slope of the corresponding linear relationship in Fig. 3b) and the detection limit of the nano-fluorescence probe for TC was 12.1 nM, which was comparable to several perovskite nanocrystals-based probes (Table S1). Through the fluorescence titration experiment of TC, the color coordinates of various TC concentrations can be calculated by importing the characteristic fluorescence spectral data under the two bands above into the CIE software. As shown in the CIE chromaticity coordinates in Fig. 3c and Table S2, the fluorescence color of the probe changed obviously from green to red after the addition of TC. The corresponding conclusion can be obtained from Fig. 3d. Under UV irradiation at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the color in the colorimetric dish notably changed with the concentration of TC, which made the detection of tetracycline more intuitive and realized the ultra-sensitive and visual detection of TC.

To evaluate the practicability of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu nanosensor for the detection of TC in complex environments, we investigated the selectivity and anti-interference of the composited probe for TC assay with coexistence of various interfering analytes. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the prepared fluorescent probe maintained excellent selectivity and anti-interference for TC with the coexistence of various cations and anions, and the intensity ratio of I
616 /I
520 was almost unchanged. Fig. 4c illustrates that in the presence of various interfering analytes, CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu can still show a sharp color change, showing a strong anti-interference performance. The selective recognition ability of the probe to other antibiotics was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4d, this composited nanoprobe was not responsive to other antibiotics except oxytetracycline. Oxytetracycline had a good response to TC, because it has a similar β-diketone structure as tetracycline.

The fluorescence sensing mechanism of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu fluorescent probe can be divided into two parts according to the response of different fluorescence channels. At first, CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu fluorescent probe can only display green fluorescence at 520 nm from CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2 (Fig. 5 a). After adding a certain concentration of TC, the emission spectra showed four typical Eu3+ characteristic peaks at 593, 616, 652, and 695 nm (Fig. 5b) because of the transition of energy from excited state (5D0) to ground state (7Fj, J = 1,2,3,4). The appearance of these peaks can be attributed to two reasons. On the one hand, the detected substance TC itself had a β-diketone structure, which can coordinate with the europium ion, and the absorption energy was transferred to europium ion through “antenna effect” [46]. On the other hand, the fluorescence of Eu3+ was quenched because of the non-radiative quenching effect of the coordination water molecules in the absence of TC. After the addition of a certain concentration of TC, TC can replace the coordinated water molecules, thereby inhibiting the effect of non-radiative quenching on Eu fluorescence. In order to verify this hypothesis, the fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 5c) of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu and CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu-TC in water and deuterium water were tested. By substituting the experimental data of fluorescence lifetime decay into Formulas 1 and 2, the change trend of coordination water can be evaluated, and the results in Table S3 show that TC can replace the coordinated water in the nanoprobe and restore the fluorescence of Eu complexes.

]

Next, with the continuous increase of tetracycline concentration, the fluorescence emission peak at 520 nm decreased (Fig. 3a). The fluorescence quenching principle can be evaluated based in the internal filtering effect (IFE) and the dynamic and static quenching effects [55]. In general, static quenching occurs when the probe forms a new non-fluorescent complex with TC, and the fluorescence lifetime of the probe remains unchanged before and after the addition of TC under the IFE effect [55]. Based on the change trend of fluorescence lifetimes in Fig. 5d and the values listed in Table S4 the fluorescence lifetimes of the CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu corresponding to different TC amounts decreased from 23.01 ns to 1.71 ns. Moreover, Fig. 5f indicates the absence of new peak in the UV–vis spectrum after the addition of TC, indicating that the dynamic quenching effect was the main inducement. However, dynamic quenching can also be divided into photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [59]. As shown in Fig. 5e, the emission spectra of CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu nanoprobe did not overlap with the absorption spectrum of TC, which can exclude the FRET effect [59,60]. The above analysis and discussion indicated that the quenching mechanism of TC by CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu was PET effect.

In this section, a portable and wearable device was prepared for rapid visualized TC assessment by fixing a layer of agarose-containing CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6@NH2@Eu nanoprobe on the surface of rubber gloves. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , the TC outside the apple samples can be semi-quantitatively detected. This gloved sensor only displayed its original green fluorescence without TC addition. However, with the help of a portable UV lamp, when the gloves gently wiped the surface of a non-TC-contaminated apple, the wearable fluorescent sensor can display various transition colors from green to red depending on the TC content on the fruit surface. Using the chromaticity analysis application (APP) in the smartphone, the fluorescence color change of gloves under different concentrations of TC can be analyzed and converted into a red-green ratio (R/G), which ensures that the color can be digitized when the TC is semi-quantitatively detected in real time. The Y axis represent the intensity ratios of fluorescence chromaticity values (R/G), while the X axis represent the TC dosages. The linear equation was obtained as Y = 0.0376 × X
1.9753 . The results confirm that the prepared portable gloved sensor enabled the qualitative and semi-quantitative visual detection of TC on fruit surfaces with good visual analysis performance.

In summary, a novel fluorescence sensor with a strong recognition ability and a good anti-interference ability for TC in the environment was prepared by doping europium complexes with perovskite nanocrystals CsPbBr3@Cs4PbBr6. In the presence of residual TC in the actual environment, TC can coordinate with rare earth europium to trigger the red fluorescence at 616 nm through AE. At the same time, considering the PET effect, the fluorescence of perovskite itself is gradually weakened. Therefore, it was selected as a reference signal to achieve multi-color fluorescence sensing of TC. In comparison with the common single fluorescence signal analysis method, the perovskite-rare earth hybrid nanoprobe had rich fluorescence color changes, which can significantly improve the resolution in visual assessment. In addition, a portable and wearable device that can realize the rapid visual detection of TC was further designed for the intelligent sensing of TC in actual fruit samples, laying a solid foundation for their wide potential applications for the detection of environmental pollutants and biomarkers.

Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft preparation. Rui Li: Visualization, Investigation, Software, Formal analysis. Zhitao Xu: Visualization, Investigation, Software. Xiangzhen Chen: Formal analysis, Data curation, Software. Yongxin Li: Software, Validation. Jian Gou: Formal analysis, Software. Lina Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ning Bi: Software, Validation. Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1904168, No. 51773052 and No. 21805071), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan (222300420047), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015), and the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University (T2021-2).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.119753.
